name: Echo Chamber
description: A dome-shaped cavern with acoustics so precise that a whisper carries from wall to wall. Stalactites hang from the ceiling in rows, each one resonating at a different pitch when struck. The sound of dripping water creates a natural rhythm. Ancient carvings on the wall depict a figure speaking, sound waves radiating outward. Coal deposits glint black among the stalactites -- ancient fuel compressed by millennia of geological patience.

responses.look: Rows of stalactites hang from the ceiling, each varying in size and thickness. Tapping them produces musical tones -- the cavern's natural pipe organ. Among the limestone formations, dark chunks of coal cling to the ceiling, deposited by ancient geological processes. Some stalactites look fragile. Others look load-bearing. Telling them apart requires more geological expertise than you possess.
responses.use: You tap a stalactite. It rings with a clear note that bounces around the chamber, echoing and re-echoing. The sound pattern reveals something -- letters, formed by the echoes: 'SERVICE.' The third guardian answer? The echo seems deliberate.
responses.take: You consider breaking off a stalactite. They've been growing for millennia. You've been alive for considerably less than that. Show some respect.
responses.default: You examine the stalactites. They hang with the patient dignity of formations that were here long before you and will be here long after.

responses.look: Ancient carvings depict a figure speaking into the chamber, with sound waves radiating outward. Below the image, text reads: 'The echo speaks truth. The Guardian's third answer is carried in resonance. Strike and listen.' It's both a clue and a warning about volume.
responses.use: You trace the carvings with your fingers. The stone is worn smooth by centuries of other fingers doing the same thing. The message is clear: use sound to find the third answer.
responses.default: You study the carvings. They're instructions, disguised as art. The kingdom's founders were annoyingly clever.

responses.look: Black chunks of ancient coal embedded in the cavern ceiling, near the base of several stalactites. The coal is old enough to have opinions about the Carboniferous period. Breaking it free would require careful extraction to avoid bringing the stalactites down.
responses.use: You could try to extract the coal, but you'd need to be careful. The stalactites around it look structural.
responses.default: You eye the coal deposits. They've been waiting patiently for someone to burn them. The anticipation is presumably palpable, if you're coal.

responses.look: Chunks of ancient coal, black and dense. They've been compressed by geological forces for millennia, which means they'll burn hot and long. Perfect for a forge.
responses.use: Coal is fuel. It needs a forge, a fire, or at minimum something that burns. By itself, it's just a very old rock.

actions.text: You carefully extract chunks of coal from around the stalactites. The coal is dense, warm to the touch, and has been waiting since before humanity invented fire. It's the geological equivalent of someone who bought concert tickets decades early. Your hands are now black. This is the least of your problems.

You tap the stalactites in sequence. Each one rings at a different pitch, and the echoes combine, bounce, layer, and reform. In the reverberating silence between notes, the echoes spell out a word: 'SERVICE.' The Guardian's third answer -- the purpose of the kingdom's bureaucracy. Service. The echo chamber stores this answer in resonance, and now it stores it in your memory. Three answers: 847, Aldric the Fastidious, Service.

echo-scream.title: Death by Acoustics
echo-scream.narratorText: You shout into the echo chamber. The dome's perfect acoustics amplify your voice. And amplify it. And amplify it. The sound wave bounces between walls, ceiling, and floor, building in intensity until the air itself vibrates at a lethal frequency. The stalactites shatter. The floor cracks. Your eardrums fail first, then everything else. The cavern has opinions about volume, and it expresses them with extreme prejudice. The echo of your scream continues for several minutes after you can no longer hear it.

stalactite-fall.title: Death by Geology
stalactite-fall.narratorText: You push a stalactite. It turns out that stalactite was load-bearing -- holding up not just itself but a considerable section of the ceiling. The geological equivalent of pulling the wrong Jenga block. The ceiling comes down with the slow majesty of a cathedral collapsing, each stalactite striking the next in a cascade of limestone and poor decisions. In retrospect, they ALL looked load-bearing. You were right to be suspicious. You were wrong to push one anyway.

dynamicDescriptions.echo_puzzle_solved: The echo chamber resonates with the fading memory of sound. The stalactites still hum faintly with the pattern that revealed the Guardian's third answer: Service. The coal deposits remain, dark and patient.
